title: Zero Trust Architecture Lab : Adversarial Validation of a Dual-Site Homelab Security Architecture
summary: Full walkthrough of architecting a dual-device Zero Trust lab aligned with NIST SP 800-207, using pfSense, Suricata, Authelia, Traefik, Splunk, and Cloudflare Zero Trust.
content:
# Zero Trust Architecture Lab: Engineering Case Study

By Karim Abdel-Nasser
---

## Overview

This document describes the design, implementation, and operational engineering of ZTA Lab - a dual-site Zero Trust Architecture lab built across two physical hosts and nine virtual machines.

The project was built to study Zero Trust principles in a hands-on, multi-site environment: how access control is enforced across multiple independent layers, how telemetry is collected and normalized from heterogeneous sources, how log pipelines are designed for signal quality rather than volume, and how detection logic is structured to produce meaningful visibility rather than alert noise.

The architecture is guided by NIST SP 800-207. No component is trusted by default. Every application access request is authenticated, authorized, and logged regardless of where it originates on the network.

---

## Design Goals

Before any component was deployed, a set of design questions shaped the architecture:

- How do you enforce identity verification at every access point without relying on network location as a trust signal?
- How do you build a log pipeline that delivers high-fidelity, enriched events without exhausting ingestion limits?
- How do you segment a multi-site environment so that a compromised host in one zone cannot directly reach management infrastructure in another?
- How do you validate that the monitoring pipeline actually captures events - without introducing noise that obscures real signal?

The answers to these questions determined the component selection, network topology, log routing design, and detection logic described in this document.

---

## Threat Model and Scope

Defining the trust boundary explicitly is what separates engineered architecture from assembled tooling.

| Assumption | Detail |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust based on network position | A host inside the network receives no inherent authorization |
| Identity must be verified per session | TOTP required on every new access session |
| Contextual signals inform access decisions | Geographic origin and device posture are evaluated per request |
| Cloudflare control plane is assumed uncompromised | Edge enforcement is treated as a trusted policy boundary |
| Physical host compromise is out of scope | Hypervisor and Windows 11 host security are not modeled |
| East-west movement between zones is restricted by default | Cross-VLAN traffic requires explicit firewall rules |

Out of scope for this version of the lab:

- Active Directory and Windows-based authentication chains
- Compromise of the Cloudflare account or tunnel token
- Supply chain attacks on Docker images or pfSense packages
- Full SOAR-based automated response

---

## Architecture

### Physical Infrastructure

The lab spans two physical Windows 11 machines running VirtualBox, connected over a home network. Each machine hosts a pfSense 2.7 firewall with Suricata IDS and a set of VMs in isolated VLANs. An IKEv2 IPsec tunnel bridges the two sites, carrying log data and application routing.

| Device | Role | Host OS | RAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Device 1 (D1) | Security gateway, SIEM host, ZTA enforcement | Windows 11 + VirtualBox | 16 GB |
| Device 2 (D2) | User network, application servers, log aggregation | Windows 11 + VirtualBox | 16 GB |

### VM Inventory

| VM | Device | VLAN | IP | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pfSense-D1 | D1 | Trunk | 192.168.10.1, 192.168.20.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| siem-vm | D1 | 10 | 192.168.10.20 | Splunk SIEM, Vector D1 |
| services-vm | D1 | 20 | 192.168.20.10 | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| pfSense-D2 | D2 | Trunk | 192.168.30.1, 192.168.40.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| appserver-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.50 | DVWA application server, auditd |
| user-vm-1 | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.10 | Simulated user workstation |
| user-vm-2 | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.20 | Simulated user workstation |
| collector-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.100 | Vector D2 log aggregator |
| kali-vm | D2 | 40 | 192.168.40.10 | Isolated test client (traffic generation) |

### Network Topology

```
                    +----------------------------------+
                    |         CLOUDFLARE EDGE          |
                    |  Custom WAF - Context Policy     |
                    |  Geo - WARP Posture - ZT Tunnel  |
                    +---------------+------------------+
                                    | cloudflared (outbound only)
                    +---------------v------------------+
                    |            DEVICE 1              |
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  | VLAN 20 - SERVICES           ||
                    |  | services-vm  192.168.20.10   ||
                    |  | Traefik - Authelia - cfrd    ||
                    |  +--------------+---------------+|
                    |  +--------------v---------------+|
                    |  | VLAN 10 - MGMT               ||
                    |  | siem-vm      192.168.10.20   ||
                    |  | Splunk - Vector D1           ||
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  pfSense-D1 - Suricata-D1        |
                    +---------------+------------------+
                                    | IPsec IKEv2 AES-256-GCM
                    +---------------v------------------+
                    |            DEVICE 2              |
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  | VLAN 30 - USERS              ||
                    |  | appserver-vm 192.168.30.50   ||
                    |  | user-vm-1    192.168.30.10   ||
                    |  | user-vm-2    192.168.30.20   ||
                    |  | collector-vm 192.168.30.100  ||
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  | VLAN 40 - ISOLATED           ||
                    |  | kali-vm      192.168.40.10   ||
                    |  +------------------------------+|
                    |  pfSense-D2 - Suricata-D2        |
                    +----------------------------------+
```

![High level.png](images/1778502976614-high-level.png)

### Network Zones and Trust Model

| Zone | VLAN | Trust Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| MGMT | 10 | Restricted | Splunk SIEM and management infrastructure |
| SERVICES | 20 | Controlled | ZTA enforcement point (Traefik + Authelia) |
| USERS | 30 | Semi-trusted | User workstations, app server, log collector |
| ISOLATED | 40 | Untrusted | Isolated zone for traffic generation and testing |

The firewall policy between zones reflects the trust model:

| Source | Destination | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| VLAN10 | Any | Allow | Management has full network reach |
| VLAN20 | VLAN10 | Allow | Traefik routes to Splunk backend |
| VLAN20 | Internet | Allow | cloudflared outbound tunnel |
| VLAN30 | VLAN10 | Allow | Log forwarding via HEC over IPsec |
| VLAN30 | Internet | Allow | General application access |
| VLAN40 | VLAN10 | Block | Isolated zone cannot reach SIEM |
| VLAN40 | VLAN20 | Block | Isolated zone cannot reach ZTA proxy |
| VLAN40 | VLAN30 | Allow | Controlled access to user zone for testing |

VLAN segmentation alone is not Zero Trust. These rules represent the network layer of a multi-layer access control model. They enforce zone boundaries but do not replace identity verification, MFA, or continuous monitoring - all of which are implemented independently.

---

## Zero Trust Access Flow

### Design Philosophy

The access control model applies three independent enforcement layers before any request reaches a backend. A failure at any layer terminates the request. No single bypass path exists because each layer operates independently of the others.

```
External Browser
  |
  v
[1] Cloudflare Edge
    - Context-aware access policy (identity + country + device posture)
    - Custom WAF rules (offensive tool User-Agent blocking)
    - Geo-restriction expression
    - Rate limiting (scoped per hostname)
  |
  v (only on policy pass)
[2] cloudflared Tunnel
    - Outbound-only connection from services-vm to Cloudflare
    - No inbound firewall ports required on Device 1
    - Traffic proxied through Cloudflare, not directly to the network
  |
  v
[3] Traefik Reverse Proxy (services-vm)
    - forwardAuth middleware on every declared route
    - Delegates authentication to Authelia before proxying
    - File-based route provider for stability
  |
  v (only on forwardAuth pass)
[4] Authelia MFA
    - TOTP required per session
    - Account lockout after 3 failed attempts within 2 minutes
    - 30-minute inactivity timeout, 8-hour maximum session
    - default_policy: deny - undeclared routes are blocked
  |
  v (only on MFA pass)
[5] Backend Application
    - Splunk (siem-vm), DVWA (appserver-vm), or Traefik dashboard
    - Never directly reachable from outside this chain
```

![Authentication & Access Flow.jpeg](images/1778502832454-authentication---access-flow.jpeg)


### Cloudflare Zero Trust - Edge Enforcement

Cloudflare serves as the outermost policy enforcement point. All external access to lab services is routed through Cloudflare's network via a cloudflared tunnel. No direct inbound connections are accepted on Device 1.

Three independent checks are evaluated per request:

Identity check - An email-based allowlist is enforced through Cloudflare Access. Requests from non-permitted identities are rejected at the edge before reaching the tunnel.

Geographic check - A custom WAF expression blocks requests originating outside the permitted country. This is an explicit expression written for this deployment, not an automatic Cloudflare feature.

Device posture check - WARP client enrollment is required. Unenrolled devices are rejected regardless of credentials or geographic origin.

Additional WAF configuration:

- Custom rules detect and block known offensive tool User-Agents (sqlmap, nikto, hydra, gobuster, masscan)
- Rate limiting blocks endpoints receiving more than 30 requests in 10 seconds, with a 1-minute ban
- All rules are scoped to specific hostnames to avoid matching internal service traffic

The access control model here is context-aware access enforcement - combining identity, geographic origin, and device posture per request. It is not full enterprise ABAC, which typically incorporates dynamic risk scoring, role metadata, and session-level attribute evaluation.

### Traefik - Policy Enforcement Point

Traefik v2.11 acts as the Policy Enforcement Point between the Cloudflare tunnel and backend applications. Every declared route uses `forwardAuth` middleware, which passes the request to Authelia for verification before proxying.

```yaml
middlewares:
  authelia:
    forwardAuth:
      address: 'http://authelia:9091/api/authz/forward-auth'
      trustForwardHeader: true
      authResponseHeaders:
        - Remote-User
        - Remote-Groups
        - Remote-Name
        - Remote-Email
```

Traefik forwards the verified identity headers to the backend after Authelia approves the request. Applications receive `Remote-User` and `Remote-Groups` headers reflecting the authenticated session.

Route definitions use the file-based provider (`--providers.file.directory`) rather than the Docker socket provider. In this deployment, the Docker socket provider intermittently failed after container restarts. File-based provider proved more stable.

### Authelia - Identity and Session Control

Authelia enforces multi-factor authentication and manages session lifecycle. Configuration uses `default_policy: deny` - any route not explicitly declared in the access control rules is blocked without an allow decision.

Session parameters:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| MFA method | TOTP (time-based one-time password) |
| Session expiration | 8 hours |
| Inactivity timeout | 30 minutes |
| Failed attempt lockout | 3 attempts within 2 minutes |
| Ban duration | 30 minutes |
| Remember me | Disabled |

### Legitimate User Access Lifecycle

A user connecting to a protected application from outside the network follows this flow:

1. Browser navigates to `splunk.<YOUR_DOMAIN>` - Cloudflare evaluates identity, country, and device posture
2. On policy pass, Cloudflare proxies the request through the cloudflared tunnel to Traefik on services-vm
3. Traefik receives the request and evaluates the route - `forwardAuth` sends an authentication check to Authelia
4. If no valid session exists, Authelia redirects the user to the login portal at `auth.<YOUR_DOMAIN>`
5. User enters credentials - Authelia verifies against the user database
6. User provides TOTP code - Authelia validates and creates a session
7. Authelia redirects back to the original URL with an authorization response header
8. Traefik receives the forwardAuth approval, attaches identity headers, and proxies the request to the Splunk backend at `192.168.10.20:8000`
9. Session persists for up to 8 hours with a 30-minute inactivity check - any inactive session requires re-authentication

No step in this flow can be bypassed from outside the chain. The application backend on siem-vm is not reachable directly - it is only accessible via the Traefik route with a valid Authelia session.

---

## Telemetry and Log Engineering

### Log Sources

The monitoring pipeline ingests events from eight distinct sources across both sites:

| Source | VM | Protocol | Port | Log Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linux syslog (auth, sudo) | user-vm-1, user-vm-2, appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | System auth events |
| auditd (syscall events) | appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | Command execution, file access |
| pfSense filterlog | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 515 | Firewall pass/block events |
| Suricata EVE JSON | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 516 | IDS alerts |
| Journald (D1 services) | siem-vm, services-vm | Vector journald | local | Service and container events |
| Docker container logs | services-vm | Vector Docker | local | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| Authelia audit log | services-vm | Vector file | local | Authentication events |
| Traefik access log | services-vm | Vector file | local | HTTP request logs |

### Pipeline Architecture

Log data flows through two Vector instances before reaching Splunk:

```
D2 Sources (VLAN30/40)
  user-vm-1        --> rsyslog UDP:514  -->+
  user-vm-2        --> rsyslog UDP:514  -->+
  appserver-vm     --> rsyslog UDP:514  -->+-- collector-vm (Vector D2)
  pfSense-D2       --> syslog UDP:515   -->+     |
  Suricata-D2      --> syslog UDP:516   -->+     | filter, parse, enrich
                                                 |
                                                 v
                                          Splunk HEC (192.168.10.20:8088)
                                          via IPsec tunnel

D1 Sources (VLAN10/20)
  siem-vm          --> journald         -->+
  services-vm      --> syslog UDP:514   -->+-- siem-vm (Vector D1)
  pfSense-D1       --> syslog UDP:515   -->+     |
  Suricata-D1      --> syslog UDP:516   -->+     | filter, parse, enrich
  Authelia log     --> file             -->+     |
  Traefik log      --> file             -->+     v
                                          Splunk HEC (localhost:8088)
```
![Logs Flow Diagram.jpeg](images/1778502902252-logs-flow-diagram.jpeg)

### Noise Reduction Strategy

Splunk Free limits ingestion to 500 MB per day. Without filtering, six VMs running normal system activity would saturate that limit before any security-relevant event is collected. The first stage of every Vector pipeline is a noise filter that drops high-volume, low-value events before they reach the enrichment layer.

Filtered event categories:

| Category | Source | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| CRON job execution | All VMs | Scheduled tasks produce no security signal |
| apt / dpkg activity | All VMs | Package manager events are routine maintenance noise |
| NTP sync (systemd-timesyncd) | All VMs | Clock synchronization has no security value here |
| NetworkManager events | All VMs | Interface events are expected and non-actionable |
| snapd activity | All VMs | Background snap refresh produces no signal |

Measured result: approximately 85% reduction in total log volume while preserving all authentication, authorization, audit, firewall, and IDS events.

```toml
[transforms.vm_filter]
  type   = "filter"
  inputs = ["d2_vms"]
  condition.type   = "vrl"
  condition.source = '''
    appname = string(.appname) ?? ""
    msg     = string(.message) ?? ""
    !(appname == "CRON" || contains(msg, "apt") || contains(msg, "dpkg") || contains(msg, "systemd-timesyncd") || contains(msg, "NetworkManager") || contains(msg, "snapd"))
  '''
```

Note on Vector 0.55 VRL syntax: `.appname ?? ""` produces an `unnecessary error coalescing operation` error because field access returns null, not a failure condition. The correct form is `string(.appname) ?? ""`. Multi-line negation expressions also fail in this version - all filter conditions must be expressed on a single line.

### Field Enrichment and Normalization

After filtering, VRL transforms parse raw syslog text into structured fields. This normalization step is what makes SPL detection queries precise and maintainable.

SSH authentication events:

```toml
if appname == "sshd" {
  .log_type = "auth"
  if contains(msg, "Failed password")  { .auth_event = "ssh_fail" }
  if contains(msg, "Accepted password") { .auth_event = "ssh_success" }
  if contains(msg, "Invalid user")     { .auth_event = "ssh_invalid" }
  parsed, err = parse_regex(msg, r'(?:Failed|Accepted) (?:password|publickey) for (?P<user>\S+) from (?P<src_ip>\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+) port (?P<port>\d+)')
  if err == null { .ssh = parsed }
}
```

Sudo privilege events:

```toml
if appname == "sudo" {
  .log_type   = "privilege"
  .auth_event = "sudo"
  parsed, err = parse_regex(msg, r'(?P<user>\S+) : TTY=\S+ ; USER=(?P<run_as>\S+) ; COMMAND=(?P<cmd>.+)')
  if err == null { .sudo = parsed }
}
```

auditd kernel events:

```toml
if appname == "audispd" || appname == "auditd" {
  .log_type = "audit"
  if contains(msg, "execve")    { .audit_event = "cmd_exec" }
  if contains(msg, "connect")   { .audit_event = "net_connect" }
  if contains(msg, "USER_AUTH") { .audit_event = "auth" }
}
```

Every event also receives site and zone metadata:

```toml
.source_site = "device2"
.lab_zone    = "users"
```

This metadata enables cross-site correlation in Splunk without ambiguity about event origin.

### Structured Fields Reference

| Field | Values | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `auth_event` | ssh_fail, ssh_success, ssh_invalid, sudo | rsyslog + VRL |
| `log_type` | auth, privilege, audit, ids_alert, firewall | VRL enrichment |
| `ssh.src_ip` | IPv4 address | VRL SSH parse |
| `ssh.user` | username string | VRL SSH parse |
| `sudo.user` | username string | VRL sudo parse |
| `sudo.run_as` | target user (typically root) | VRL sudo parse |
| `sudo.cmd` | full command path | VRL sudo parse |
| `audit_event` | cmd_exec, net_connect, auth | VRL auditd parse |
| `alert_sig` | Suricata rule name | VRL EVE JSON parse |
| `alert_severity` | 1-3 (Suricata severity) | VRL EVE JSON parse |
| `firewall.src_ip` | IPv4 address | VRL filterlog parse |
| `firewall.dst_ip` | IPv4 address | VRL filterlog parse |
| `firewall.action` | pass, block | VRL filterlog parse |
| `source_site` | device1, device2 | VRL metadata tag |
| `lab_zone` | mgmt, services, users | VRL metadata tag |

### Splunk HEC Delivery

Vector delivers enriched events to Splunk via HEC (HTTP Event Collector) on port 8088. D2 events travel over the IPsec tunnel from collector-vm to siem-vm. D1 events are delivered to HEC on localhost.

```toml
[sinks.splunk_hec]
  type       = "splunk_hec_logs"
  inputs     = ["vm_enrich"]
  endpoint   = "http://192.168.10.20:8088"
  token      = "<YOUR_HEC_TOKEN>"
  index      = "main"
  sourcetype = "syslog"
  [sinks.splunk_hec.encoding]
    codec = "json"
```

---

## Detection and Monitoring Design

### Detection Philosophy

The SPL rules below are designed as prototype monitoring logic - structured to detect meaningful security events with low false-positive rates. They are examples of how the enriched field schema enables precise, readable detection queries. They are not presented as a formally validated or production-certified detection library.

The goal in designing these rules was signal quality over alert volume. A rule that produces one high-confidence alert per relevant event is more operationally useful than one that fires on every related log line.

### SSH Brute Force Detection - T1110.001

Counts failed SSH attempts per source IP and host. Threshold is set at five failures to balance sensitivity against noise from misconfigured clients.

```spl
index=main auth_event=ssh_fail
| stats count as attempts by ssh.src_ip, ssh.user, host
| where attempts >= 5
| eval severity=if(attempts>=20, "CRITICAL", "HIGH")
| eval source_zone=case(
    like('ssh.src_ip', "192.168.40.%"), "VLAN40-ISOLATED",
    like('ssh.src_ip', "192.168.30.%"), "VLAN30-USERS",
    true(), "EXTERNAL")
| sort -attempts
| table ssh.src_ip, ssh.user, host, attempts, severity, source_zone
```

The `source_zone` field helps distinguish misconfigured internal clients (VLAN30) from potentially adversarial traffic (VLAN40).

### Credential Reuse After Failure - T1110 -> T1078

Correlates failure and success events from the same source. The combination indicates a credential was successfully obtained after repeated attempts.

```spl
index=main (auth_event=ssh_fail OR auth_event=ssh_success)
| rex field=_raw "from (?P<src_ip>\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)"
| eval action=if(auth_event="ssh_fail", "FAIL", "SUCCESS")
| stats values(action) as actions, count by src_ip, host
| where like(mvjoin(actions, ","), "%FAIL%")
  AND like(mvjoin(actions, ","), "%SUCCESS%")
| eval severity="HIGH"
```

### Suricata IDS Alert Monitoring

Surfaces Suricata severity-1 alerts with source/destination context and rule classification.

```spl
index=main log_type=ids_alert alert_severity=1
| stats count by src_ip, dst_ip, alert_sig, alert_category
| sort -count
```

### Privilege Escalation via Sudo - T1548.003

Detects any command executed as root via sudo, using the structured `sudo.*` fields produced by VRL enrichment.

```spl
index=main auth_event=sudo sudo.run_as=root
| stats count by sudo.user, sudo.cmd, host
| eval severity="HIGH"
```

The value of the enriched fields is visible here: without VRL parsing, this query would require a complex regex against raw syslog text. With enrichment, it is a precise field filter.

### Off-Hours Authentication

Flags successful authentications outside expected working hours. Uses server-local time from the event timestamp.

```spl
index=main auth_event=ssh_success
| eval hour=strftime(_time, "%H")
| where hour < "07" OR hour > "22"
| table _time, ssh.user, ssh.src_ip, host, hour
| eval severity="MEDIUM"
```

### auditd Command Execution Visibility - T1059.004

Surfaces execution of shells, interpreters, and network utilities captured by auditd's configured execve syscall rules.

```spl
index=main log_type=audit audit_event=cmd_exec
| rex field=message "exe=\"(?P<executable>[^\"]+)\""
| where executable IN ("/bin/bash", "/bin/sh", "/usr/bin/python3",
                       "/bin/nc", "/usr/bin/curl", "/usr/bin/wget")
| stats count by executable, host
```

### Firewall Cross-Zone Visibility

Tracks firewall pass and block events at zone boundaries. Useful for identifying unexpected traffic patterns between trust zones.

```spl
index=main log_type=firewall
| eval src_zone=case(
    like(firewall.src_ip, "192.168.40.%"), "VLAN40-ISOLATED",
    like(firewall.src_ip, "192.168.30.%"), "VLAN30-USERS",
    like(firewall.src_ip, "192.168.20.%"), "VLAN20-SERVICES",
    like(firewall.src_ip, "192.168.10.%"), "VLAN10-MGMT",
    true(), "EXTERNAL")
| eval dst_zone=case(
    like(firewall.dst_ip, "192.168.10.%"), "VLAN10-MGMT",
    like(firewall.dst_ip, "192.168.20.%"), "VLAN20-SERVICES",
    like(firewall.dst_ip, "192.168.30.%"), "VLAN30-USERS",
    true(), "OTHER")
| stats count by src_zone, dst_zone, firewall.action
| sort -count
```

### Multi-Stage Activity Correlation

Correlates multiple event types on the same host within a time window. Produces a behavioral summary rather than a single-event alert, which is more useful for triage.

```spl
index=main earliest=-60m
| eval stage=case(
    log_type="ids_alert" AND like(alert_sig, "%scan%"), "recon",
    auth_event="ssh_fail",                               "brute_force",
    auth_event="ssh_success",                            "auth_success",
    auth_event="sudo",                                   "privilege",
    log_type="audit" AND like(message, "%shadow%"),      "sensitive_file",
    log_type="audit" AND like(message, "%crontab%"),     "persistence",
    true(), "other")
| where stage != "other"
| stats values(stage) as observed_stages, count by host
| eval stage_count=mvcount(observed_stages)
| where stage_count >= 2
| table host, observed_stages, stage_count
```

### Threat Intelligence Lookup

Matches observed source IPs against an external blocklist loaded as a Splunk lookup table.

```spl
index=main
| rex field=_raw "from (?P<src_ip>\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+)"
| lookup threat_intel ip as src_ip OUTPUT threat_type
| where isnotnull(threat_type)
| table _time, src_ip, threat_type, host, log_type
```

### SOC Dashboard Structure

The monitoring dashboard is organized into four panels:

| Panel | Query Basis | Refresh |
|---|---|---|
| SSH failure count (1h) | `auth_event=ssh_fail` count | 30 seconds |
| Suricata alert count (1h) | `log_type=ids_alert` count | 30 seconds |
| Privilege escalation events (24h) | `auth_event=sudo sudo.run_as=root` count | 5 minutes |
| Cross-zone firewall activity | `log_type=firewall` by zone pair | 5 minutes |
| Authentication timeline | `auth_event` timechart by type | 30 seconds |
| Active alert detail table | `log_type=ids_alert` detail | 30 seconds |

---

## Traffic Generation and Pipeline Validation

To confirm that log sources, Vector pipelines, and Splunk detection rules were producing correct output, limited internal traffic generation was used during development and testing.

This is not a formal security evaluation. It is a pipeline validation exercise - the equivalent of injecting known test events to confirm that the end-to-end telemetry chain produces the expected output in Splunk.

### Validation Approach

For each log source and detection rule, the validation process followed three steps:

1. Generate a known event from the relevant source (for example, a failed SSH login attempt, a sudo command, or a Suricata-triggering network scan)
2. Confirm the event appears in Vector logs with the expected enriched fields
3. Confirm the corresponding SPL rule produces the expected output in Splunk

This approach confirmed that the VRL enrichment, HEC delivery, and SPL field references were all correctly aligned. It also revealed several pipeline issues that required correction before the monitoring stack was functional.

### Sample Validation Commands

```bash
# Inject a synthetic auth failure to test rsyslog -> Vector -> Splunk pipeline
logger -p auth.warning -t sshd \
  "Failed password for root from 192.168.40.10 port 22 ssh2"

# Verify event appears in Splunk within 30 seconds
# Expected: index=main auth_event=ssh_fail ssh.src_ip=192.168.40.10

# Confirm HEC is healthy before starting Vector
curl -s http://192.168.10.20:8088/services/collector/health
# Expected: {"text":"HEC is healthy","code":17}

# Monitor Vector throughput in real time
sudo vector top
```

The kali-vm (VLAN40) was used to generate controlled test traffic - nmap scans to test Suricata and pfSense filterlog capture, SSH attempts to test rsyslog and auth detection rules, and basic web requests to test Traefik access log ingestion. These were targeted and limited in scope, used to confirm that each pipeline segment was producing correctly structured events.

### Access Control Verification

As part of confirming the architecture matched its intended design, several access paths were tested from the isolated zone:

| Attempted Access | Expected Result | Observed Result |
|---|---|---|
| VLAN40 host to siem-vm (192.168.10.20) | Connection blocked by firewall | Request timed out - confirmed |
| VLAN40 host to services-vm (192.168.20.10) | Connection blocked by firewall | Request timed out - confirmed |
| External access without WARP enrollment | Rejected by Cloudflare | Cloudflare 403 response - confirmed |
| Authelia login without valid TOTP | Session rejected | Login refused at MFA step - confirmed |
| Route with no Authelia policy | Blocked by default_policy: deny | Traefik 403 response - confirmed |

These confirmations validated that the architecture behaved as designed - not as a claim of adversarial resilience, but as a functional check that each control was active and correctly configured.

---

## NIST SP 800-207 Principles Applied

| Principle | Implementation |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust | All externally initiated requests pass through Cloudflare, Traefik, and Authelia regardless of network source |
| All communication secured | HTTPS via Traefik TLS, AES-256-GCM IPsec for inter-site traffic, outbound-only cloudflared tunnel |
| Access granted per session | TOTP required per session, 8-hour maximum, 30-minute inactivity timeout |
| Dynamic policy enforcement | Identity, geographic origin, and device posture evaluated per request at the Cloudflare layer |
| Continuous monitoring | Vector + Splunk provide real-time enriched event collection across all zones |
| Least privilege | Firewall rules default to deny between zones; access requires explicit policy |

---

## Operational Engineering Challenges

These are issues encountered during deployment, included because they reflect real infrastructure behavior rather than ideal documentation.

### Cloudflare WAF Self-Rate-Limiting

A rate limiting rule using `http.host contains "<DOMAIN>"` also matched Splunk's internal API calls (approximately 50 requests per page load), causing the dashboard to trigger its own rate limit. The fix was to scope rate limiting rules to specific application hostnames rather than the root domain.

### Vector Startup Race Condition

Vector initializing before Splunk's HEC endpoint was ready produced sustained `Healthcheck timed out` errors. A startup wait loop polling HEC health before starting Vector resolved this reliably.

```bash
until curl -sf http://192.168.10.20:8088/services/collector/health \
  2>/dev/null | grep -q "HEC is healthy"; do
  sleep 10
done
sudo systemctl restart vector
```

### rsyslog Silent Failure on Fresh Ubuntu Installs

The `imjournal` module fails on startup when `/var/lib/rsyslog/` does not exist. `systemctl status rsyslog` reports active regardless of whether log forwarding is actually working.

```bash
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/rsyslog
sudo chown syslog:syslog /var/lib/rsyslog
sudo systemctl restart rsyslog
```

This directory must be created before deploying rsyslog on any fresh Ubuntu install. The failure is silent and produces no diagnostic output.

### Vector VRL Syntax Constraints

Two specific constraints in Vector 0.55 affected pipeline development:

`.field ?? ""` throws `unnecessary error coalescing operation` - field access returns null, not an error. The `??` operator applies only to operations that can fail. Correct form: `string(.field) ?? ""`.

Multi-line negation expressions fail at parse time. All filter conditions must be expressed on a single line regardless of complexity.

### Suricata EVE JSON Forwarding Reset

Suricata resets its EVE JSON syslog destination after every service restart or pfSense GUI update. This appears to be a known pfSense package behavior. After any Suricata restart, the remote syslog server and port must be manually re-entered in the WebGUI. This caused silent alert forwarding failures several times during testing.

### IPsec Validation Method

pfSense's built-in diagnostic ping uses locally generated traffic that bypasses IPsec routing, making it unsuitable for tunnel connectivity validation. VM-to-VM ping tests are required to confirm that the IPsec tunnel is carrying actual traffic correctly.

### Traefik Docker Provider Instability

In this deployment, the Docker socket provider intermittently produced routes that appeared configured in the Traefik dashboard but served no traffic after container restarts. File-based provider with `--providers.file.directory` proved more reliable throughout the lab's lifecycle.

### Authelia Configuration File Corruption

In certain terminal environments, writing YAML configuration via heredoc converts the string `server.host` into a hyperlink, silently corrupting the file. Writing configuration files via Python's `open().write()` avoids this issue entirely.

---

## Known Limitations

| Limitation | Impact |
|---|---|
| No EDR/XDR platform | Endpoint visibility relies on auditd syscall rules and rsyslog |
| Linux-only environment | No Windows attack surface - Active Directory, NTLM, and Kerberos are untested |
| No Active Directory | Authentication is local-account and SSH based only |
| No SOAR automation | Alert response is manual - detection fires but no automated containment |
| Splunk Free ingestion cap | 500 MB/day constrains log retention and high-volume test scenarios |
| No hardware TAP or SPAN | Network visibility is IDS-based only - no full packet capture |
| VirtualBox networking overhead | Promiscuous mode and internal VLAN tagging introduces latency |
| Detection rules are prototypes | SPL rules have not been tuned against production-volume log data |

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Physical machines | 2 |
| Virtual machines | 9 |
| VLANs / trust zones | 4 |
| Log sources integrated | 8 |

| Prototype SPL detection rules | 8 |
| Log volume reduction via noise filtering | ~85% |
| ZTA access control layers | 3 independent (Cloudflare, Traefik, Authelia) |
| Cross-site delivery method | Splunk HEC over IPsec tunnel |

## Project Value and Conclusions

This project demonstrates practical implementation across five engineering domains that are often treated as separate disciplines:

Network architecture - Designing a segmented multi-site environment where trust zones are enforced at the firewall layer rather than assumed from network position.

Identity and access control - Building a three-layer access enforcement chain (Cloudflare context policy, Traefik forwardAuth, Authelia MFA) that applies consistently to every application route regardless of source.

Telemetry engineering - Designing a log pipeline that aggregates heterogeneous sources, normalizes them into a consistent field schema, filters noise systematically, and delivers enriched events to a central SIEM across a VPN tunnel.

Detection design - Writing SPL rules against structured fields rather than raw text, structured to detect behavioral patterns across multiple event types rather than individual log lines.

Operational troubleshooting - Diagnosing infrastructure failures across a multi-component stack where failures are often silent and root causes require cross-layer investigation.

The core architectural conclusion from building this lab is that Zero Trust is a design commitment across every layer, not a property of any individual component. Cloudflare alone enforces edge policy but has no visibility into internal routing. Authelia alone manages sessions but cannot enforce network segmentation. pfSense alone segments the network but cannot verify identity. The access model only functions as intended when all layers are correctly connected and operating together.

*Stack: Splunk 9.3.2 - Vector 0.55.0 - Traefik v2.11 - Authelia - pfSense 2.7 - Suricata - Cloudflare Zero Trust - Docker - Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 - VirtualBox 7.x*

*Zero Trust Architecture Lab*
